statement: Social security coordination between Iceland and EU member states is governed by EU Regulation 883/2004, incorporated into the EEA Agreement. This allows workers to aggregate insurance periods across countries for pension, unemployment, and healthcare entitlements. However, EEA implementation lags behind intra-EU coordination in several areas: processing times for S1/E106 healthcare forms average 4–8 weeks for EEA states versus 1–2 weeks within the EU; pension aggregation claims from Iceland take approximately 6–12 months longer than intra-EU transfers; and Iceland is not part of the Electronic Exchange of Social Security Information (EESSI) system, requiring paper-based processes for cross-border claims.
source_name: Tryggingastofnun ríkisins (Social Insurance Administration); EU — EESSI system documentation
caveats: Processing time differences are estimates from practitioner reports, not official benchmarks. Iceland's small administration handles a relatively low volume of cross-border cases. EESSI integration is planned but not yet implemented for EEA states.

related_entries.statement: Iceland already participates in the free movement of workers through the EEA Agreement, which has been in force since 1994. EEA nationals have the right to live and work in Iceland without work permits, and Icelanders have reciprocal rights across the EU/EEA. EU membership would not create new free movement rights beyond what already exists under the EEA framework. However, EU membership would give Iceland voting rights in shaping the rules governing free movement, whereas under the EEA Iceland implements EU single market legislation without a formal vote in the Council or European Parliament.

Iceland's labour market regulation differs from the EU norm in several key respects. Union density is approximately 90% (vs EU average ~23%), and wages are set primarily through centralised collective agreements between ASÍ and SA rather than through legislation or sectoral bargaining. There is no statutory minimum wage — the collective agreement minimum functions as the de facto floor. Iceland has already transposed most EU employment directives through the EEA, including working time, equal treatment, and posted workers rules. Key areas where EU membership could bring additional regulation include works councils (European Works Council Directive has limited EEA application), co-determination, and the proposed EU minimum wage directive.
